did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper implemented a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) deep learning model to classify seven classes of skin lesions (dermatology task) using the HAM10000 dataset. The model achieved an average performance score of 0.98 and was integrated into a mobile application for skin cancer detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation of a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model and its deployment into a mobile application using Expo React Native. It reports specific results from model training on the HAM10000 dataset (e.g., accuracy of 0.980824) and application testing using black box testing (100% success rate), which constitutes new technical results rather than a summary of prior literature.

title: Implementasi Model Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) pada Aplikasi Deteksi Kanker Kulit Menggunakan Expo React Native
journal: Building of Informatics, Technology and Science (BITS)
brief_summary: This study developed a mobile skin cancer detection application using Expo React Native and a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model to classify seven classes of skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset. The CNN model was trained with data augmentation and oversampling, achieving an average performance score of 0.98 across metrics including accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score. The model was converted to TensorFlow JS format and integrated into the mobile application for real-time prediction. Application functionality was validated using black box testing, which resulted in a 100% success rate across all tested features such as image selection, camera capture, and prediction. The research demonstrates that the implemented deep learning model is feasible for deployment in mobile applications for early skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.target: Seven classes of skin lesions (Actinic keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevi, Vascular lesions) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) using a custom CNN architecture. The output is an array of seven elements representing probabilities for each class, with the highest probability index assigned as the predicted label. The task is explicitly described as classifying seven classes of skin lesions.

datasets_used.name: ISIC HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 10,015 dermatoscopic images of skin lesions, used as the primary data source for training and evaluating the CNN model.

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN model with data augmentation and oversampling techniques.
use.testing: Used for evaluating model performance metrics (accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score, etc.).
use.other: Images from this dataset were also used in application black-box testing to verify prediction functionality.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score, etc.) and application functionality was validated using black box testing.

HAM10000.experiment: CNN Model Evaluation (with data augmentation and oversampling)
HAM10000.notes: The model achieved an average performance score of 0.98. The confusion matrix indicated stable results.

Mobile Application (Expo React Native).notes: All four tested parts (select image, open camera, predict image, delete image) demonstrated that functionality and features work well.

Mobile Application Performance (Latency).notes: Prediction time varied based on photo source, size, and object. Images from the HAM10000 dataset inputted from the gallery had significantly faster prediction times compared to photos taken from the camera or non-lesion skin images.

architecture_details.description: A custom CNN architecture consisting of convolutional layers for feature extraction, max pooling layers for data size reduction, batch normalization for input normalization between layers, and a flatten layer to convert arrays into one-dimensional vectors.

classification_or_output_head.output_format: Array of seven elements representing probabilities for each of the seven skin lesion classes
classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: Index with the highest probability is assigned as the predicted label

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing (resizing input images to match model input size)
Normalization (dividing pixel values by 255.0 to normalize to range [0,1])
Base64 encoding (converting input image to base64 string)
Tensor decoding (decoding image into image tensor using bilinear interpolation)
Tensor conversion (converting normalized image into 4D tensor)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used during model training on the HAM10000 dataset. Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation, flipping, zoom ranges) were not reported in the text.
Used during model training to handle class imbalance. Specific sampling ratios or methods (e.g., SMOTE, random oversampling) were not reported in the text.

conclusions: The custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model achieved high performance with an average score of 0.98 across metrics including accuracy (0.980824), recall (0.980698), precision (0.981622), and F1-score (0.980474) on the HAM10000 dataset.
The CNN model is feasible and ready for implementation in mobile applications for skin cancer detection.
The developed skin cancer detection application using Expo React Native successfully implemented the deep learning model and functioned correctly.
Black box testing of the application resulted in a 100% success rate, confirming that all features (select image, open camera, predict image, and delete image) operated as intended.

Prediction latency varied significantly based on the image source and content; images from the HAM10000 dataset inputted via the gallery had faster prediction times (approx. 1000ms) compared to photos taken from the camera or non-lesion skin images (approx. 25,000-29,000ms).
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have class imbalance and demographic biases; no external validation on independent clinical datasets was performed.
The custom CNN architecture details (number of layers, filter sizes, parameter counts) are not explicitly reported, limiting reproducibility.
Hyperparameters such as optimizer, learning rate, loss function, and training schedule are missing from the report.

The reported average performance score of 0.98 is unusually high for a multi-class skin lesion classification task on HAM10000, suggesting potential data leakage or overly optimistic evaluation metrics without rigorous cross-validation.
Application testing was limited to black-box functional testing (100% success rate) and latency measurements; no clinical sensitivity/specificity analysis or user study was conducted.
Prediction latency varied significantly (approx. 1s for pre-processed HAM10000 images vs. 25-29s for camera/gallery inputs), indicating poor real-time usability for non-standard inputs.
The model was converted to TensorFlow JS for mobile deployment, but the impact of quantization or conversion on model accuracy was not evaluated.
The study does not address the clinical risk of false negatives, which is critical for skin cancer detection applications.
